jokes.punchline: 'Feel free to toss yourself into the mix' — Peterman casually suggests Elaine write herself into his romantic escapades, which is both an inappropriate workplace suggestion and a sign of how he views her as a narrative resource
jokes.explanation: Strong Peterman closer for the scene. 'Go forth and create' is appropriately pompous, then the pivot to suggesting Elaine include herself in his romantic chapters is wonderfully inappropriate and Peterman-specific.

jokes.text: Hey, Van B Boys. — So, Mr. Costanza, did you get my scholarship back? — Now, fellas, fellas, easy. Wouldn't wanna beat up on one of your own. — Is that right? — Then why don't you flash us the sign?
jokes.setup: George confronts the Van Buren Boys while trying to avoid violence
jokes.punchline: The gang demands he prove membership by flashing the sign — the eight-finger Van Buren sign — and the audience knows George doesn't know it
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the final act's George scene. The audience is ahead of George — we know what the sign is (eight fingers) and we're watching to see if George will figure it out. Sustained cringe comedy.

jokes.text: That's not the sign. — It was when I was banging.
jokes.setup: George attempts to flash the Van Buren Boys' secret sign and gets it wrong
jokes.punchline: 'It was when I was banging' — George's excuse for the wrong sign is that it was the correct sign in his era, implying he's claiming to be an original Van Buren Boy from some earlier decade
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's very best jokes. George, caught with the wrong sign, instantly constructs a cover story that implies he's a veteran member from an era when the sign was different. 'It was when I was banging' — the casual slang, the historical claim, and the total commitment to the lie — is pure George Costanza at his best. The economy is outstanding (six words). Extremely quotable and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: All right, if you are really one of us, let's see you take the wallet off the next guy who walks by. — Love to.
jokes.setup: The Van Buren Boys demand George prove his membership by pickpocketing the next passerby
jokes.punchline: 'Love to' — George's instant cheerful agreement to commit a crime under social pressure, delivered with characteristic enthusiasm for survival
jokes.explanation: George's enthusiastic compliance ('Love to') is a perfect character beat — he has no moral objection, only self-preservation motivation. The phrase is very George: simultaneously eager and self-serving.

jokes.text: And after college I got my master's at the Sorbonne. — Sorbonne. Oh, hey, that's in Paris.
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents are meeting Ellen and she's telling them about her education
jokes.punchline: Morty identifies 'The Sorbonne' as being 'in Paris' — providing the most basic possible geographical annotation as if it's useful information, or as if he's not sure and just figured it out
jokes.explanation: Good Morty Seinfeld character beat — the proud explanation of the Sorbonne's location as if he's providing genuinely useful context is perfectly in his register of earnest slightly-missing-the-point commentary.

jokes.text: Jerry, your parking meter is about to expire. — Don't get up. I've got change.
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents are evaluating Ellen; Ellen notices Jerry's meter is expiring
jokes.punchline: Morty immediately jumps to handle it himself — not letting Jerry get up, treating the meter as something he can deal with — which may signal his discomfort with the dinner or simply his characteristic helpfulness
jokes.explanation: Minor character beat — Morty jumping up to handle the meter is in character but this is primarily a setup beat for the George-pickpocketing sequence rather than a standalone joke. Mild.

jokes.text: Jerry, she is fantastic. — I knew it. I'm not crazy. — She's so sweet. — And she's got some body on her. — And smart, like a computer. — And so much personality. But it doesn't matter what we think. Do you like her?
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents give their verdict on Ellen
jokes.punchline: They're effusively positive, making Jerry doubt himself — 'she's got some body on her' from Morty is also an unexpected detail from a father about his son's girlfriend
jokes.explanation: The parents' verdict being overwhelmingly positive reverses Jerry's expected outcome — he brought them in to resolve doubt, and they've made it worse. Morty's 'she's got some body on her' is a funny specific from a father figure. The final 'but do you like her?' reframes the whole scene.

jokes.text: Now I'm not so sure.
jokes.setup: His parents have just given an overwhelmingly positive review of Ellen
jokes.punchline: Their approval makes Jerry less sure — the joke being that Jerry's contrarianism means his parents liking her is a mark against Ellen
jokes.explanation: Sharp character beat. Jerry's 'now I'm not so sure' after rave parental reviews is perfectly Seinfeldian — any external approval becomes a reason to doubt. Economy is outstanding.

jokes.text: She's 10 times better than that awful Amber girl that you were with. — Yeah, Amber. I wonder if she's back from Vegas.
jokes.setup: Jerry's mother praises Ellen by comparing her favorably to an ex
jokes.punchline: Jerry immediately becomes distracted thinking about Amber — suggesting he's now going to pursue his ex rather than stay with the wonderful Ellen
jokes.explanation: Perfect Seinfeld character comedy. The mention of Amber was meant to praise Ellen; instead it reminds Jerry of Amber and makes him want to pursue her. It neatly encapsulates the show's central thesis about Jerry's impossibility as a romantic partner and sets up the Ellen exit.

jokes.text: How are your folks? — Trying to pick out a new couch. You don't wanna know. — Give me your wallet or I'll spill your guts right here on the street.
jokes.setup: George is trying to get Morty's wallet while maintaining a friendly conversation
jokes.punchline: After exchanging pleasantries about couches, George threatens to 'spill your guts' — the tonal whiplash from normal small talk to gang threat is the joke
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash is the joke — George sliding from 'how are your folks?' and couch small talk directly into 'I'll spill your guts' is perfectly absurd. The mundane couch detail ('you don't wanna know') makes the threat even funnier by contrast.

jokes.text: What did you say? — Come on, hurry up, old man. — I'm an animal.
jokes.setup: George is threatening Morty Seinfeld in front of his wife and the Van Buren Boys
jokes.punchline: 'I'm an animal' — George's self-description as an animal while mugging an elderly man named Morty Seinfeld is a commitment to the bit that is both ridiculous and very George
jokes.explanation: Outstanding closing character beat. 'I'm an animal' is a great George line — self-aggrandizing, completely unearned, delivered in the context of threatening a sweet old man. The phrase has a classic Costanza quality: he genuinely believes it for the three seconds he says it. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: You're being very rude. Come on, Morty. — Please, they're gonna hit me. — What? — George Costanza, what is the matter with you?
jokes.setup: George is mugging the Seinfelds while Van Buren Boys watch
jokes.punchline: Helen Seinfeld's indignant 'you're being very rude' treats a mugging as a social infraction; George's 'please, they're gonna hit me' breaks character entirely and is immediately called out by Helen
jokes.explanation: Helen treating 'I'll spill your guts' as rudeness is perfect — her moral register is social etiquette, not violence. George immediately breaking down and saying 'please, they're gonna hit me' is a great character collapse. 'George Costanza, what is the matter with you?' is a wonderful button — maternal disapproval cutting through gang dynamics. The use of his full name seals it.

jokes.text: Tell your parents we said hi.
jokes.setup: George has completely failed at pickpocketing and his cover is blown
jokes.punchline: The Van Buren Boys leave with a polite 'tell your parents we said hi' — a completely normal social farewell from a street gang after a botched mugging
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best closing line. After a tense gang confrontation, the Van Buren Boys sign off with 'tell your parents we said hi' — a perfectly cordial social farewell that completely deflates any remaining menace. The economy is perfect (six words). The tonal whiplash from gang threat to social courtesy is expertly timed. Extremely quotable and a satisfying button on the entire Van Buren Boys plot.

tmdb_overview: Gang members protest George's handling of a scholarship named for Susan; some find Jerry's new girlfriend objectionable; Peterman wants Kramer's life experiences for his own autobiography, written by Elaine.
insight: Gang initiation satire sustains 74 jokes across 22 minutes despite modest 77 impact score.
